title: Epistemology
content: Epistemology is the study of knowledge. The traditional analysis treats knowledge as justified true belief: a subject S knows that p iff p is true, S believes that p, and S is justified in believing that p. Gettier cases famously challenge this analysis by exhibiting situations in which the conditions are met but intuition denies that the subject knows.

title: Categorical Imperative
content: Kant's categorical imperative requires that one act only according to that maxim by which one can at the same time will that it should become a universal law. The principle is categorical because it applies unconditionally, without reference to any further end, and contrasts with hypothetical imperatives that prescribe means to a desired end.

title: Free Will
content: The free-will debate concerns whether agents can be the genuine source of their actions. Compatibilists argue that free will is consistent with causal determinism so long as the agent acts on her own desires without external coercion. Libertarians deny this, holding that genuine freedom requires that the agent could have done otherwise in exactly the same circumstances.

title: Phenomenal Consciousness
content: The hard problem of consciousness, articulated by David Chalmers, asks why physical processing gives rise to subjective experience at all. Even a complete functional account of cognition seems to leave unexplained why there is something it is like to have those states — the qualia of red, of pain, of taste.

title: Utilitarianism
content: Utilitarianism, developed by Bentham and refined by Mill, holds that actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness, wrong as they tend to produce the reverse. Mill distinguishes higher from lower pleasures and argues that competent judges would prefer the cultivation of intellectual and moral capacities to mere sensory satisfaction.

title: Linear Algebra
content: A vector space over a field F is a set V with addition and scalar multiplication satisfying eight axioms: associativity and commutativity of addition, identity and inverse for addition, compatibility of scalar multiplication, identity scalar, and two distributive laws. A linear map between vector spaces preserves both operations: T(au + bv) = a T(u) + b T(v).

title: Eigenvalues and Eigenvectors
content: Given a square matrix A, an eigenvector v with eigenvalue lambda satisfies A v = lambda v with v nonzero. Eigenvalues are roots of the characteristic polynomial det(A - lambda I) = 0. They reveal invariant directions of the linear map and underlie spectral decomposition, principal-component analysis, and the stability of dynamical systems.

title: Calculus — Fundamental Theorem
content: The fundamental theorem of calculus links differentiation and integration. Part one: if f is continuous on [a,b] and F(x) = integral from a to x of f(t) dt, then F'(x) = f(x). Part two: if F is an antiderivative of f on [a,b], then integral from a to b of f(x) dx equals F(b) - F(a). The theorem turns evaluation of definite integrals into a search for antiderivatives.

title: Set Theory
content: Zermelo-Fraenkel set theory with the axiom of choice (ZFC) provides the standard foundation for modern mathematics. Its axioms include extensionality (sets are equal iff they have the same members), pairing, union, power set, infinity, replacement, foundation, and choice. The cumulative hierarchy V = union over ordinals alpha of V_alpha builds every set from the empty set by iterated power-set operations.

title: Discrete Probability
content: Bayes' theorem relates conditional probabilities: P(A|B) = P(B|A) P(A) / P(B). Read as an updating rule, it converts a prior P(A) into a posterior P(A|B) by reweighting with the likelihood ratio. The theorem underwrites Bayesian inference, where beliefs are revised in light of evidence, and is fundamental to modern statistics, machine learning, and decision theory.

title: Photosynthesis
section: Light reactions and the Calvin cycle
content: Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy stored as glucose. In plants the light reactions occur in the thylakoid membrane: photosystems II and I split water and excite electrons, generating ATP and NADPH while releasing oxygen. The Calvin cycle then uses ATP and NADPH in the stroma to fix carbon dioxide via the enzyme RuBisCO into three-carbon sugars.

title: Newton's Laws of Motion
content: Newton's three laws of motion: (1) a body remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted on by a net external force; (2) the net force equals mass times acceleration, F = m a; (3) for every action there is an equal and opposite reaction. Together they ground classical mechanics and remain accurate at speeds and scales where relativistic and quantum corrections are negligible.

title: DNA Replication
content: DNA replication is semiconservative: each daughter molecule contains one parental and one newly synthesized strand. Helicase unwinds the double helix; primase lays down RNA primers; DNA polymerase extends the leading strand continuously and the lagging strand in Okazaki fragments later joined by ligase. Proofreading by polymerase exonuclease activity keeps the per-base error rate near 10^-9.

title: Second Law of Thermodynamics
content: The second law of thermodynamics states that the total entropy of an isolated system never decreases over time and is constant only in reversible processes. It explains why heat flows from hot to cold spontaneously, why no engine cyclically converts heat into work with perfect efficiency, and why time appears to have a direction at the macroscopic scale.

title: Cellular Respiration
content: Cellular respiration extracts energy from glucose in three stages. Glycolysis in the cytoplasm splits glucose into two pyruvate molecules, yielding 2 ATP and 2 NADH. The Krebs cycle in the mitochondrial matrix oxidizes acetyl-CoA, producing CO2, NADH, FADH2, and a small amount of ATP. Oxidative phosphorylation uses the electron transport chain and chemiosmosis to generate the bulk of cellular ATP.

title: Big-O Notation
content: Big-O notation describes the asymptotic upper bound on a function's growth. f(n) = O(g(n)) iff there exist positive constants c and n0 such that |f(n)| <= c |g(n)| for all n >= n0. In algorithm analysis it captures how runtime or memory scales with input size; common bounds include O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2), and O(2^n).

title: Binary Search Tree
content: A binary search tree (BST) stores keys so that for every node, the left subtree's keys are less and the right subtree's keys are greater. Search, insert, and delete run in O(h) time where h is the tree height — O(log n) for balanced trees but O(n) in the degenerate case. Self-balancing variants (AVL, red-black) maintain O(log n) by rotating after modifications.

title: TCP/IP
content: The TCP/IP stack layers responsibilities: link layer moves frames over physical media; the internet layer (IP) routes packets between hosts; the transport layer offers connectionless (UDP) or reliable, ordered, congestion-controlled (TCP) byte streams; the application layer (HTTP, SMTP, DNS, ...) defines protocols on top. Each layer adds and strips its own header on send and receive.

title: Backpropagation
section: Gradient descent through a neural network
content: Backpropagation computes gradients of a neural network's loss with respect to every weight by the chain rule, propagating partial derivatives backward from the output. Combined with stochastic gradient descent (or Adam, RMSProp, etc.), it is the workhorse training algorithm for deep models. The method requires the loss and activations to be differentiable almost everywhere.

title: Public-Key Cryptography
content: Public-key cryptography uses a key pair: a public key for encryption (or signature verification) and a private key for decryption (or signing). RSA bases security on the hardness of factoring large semiprimes; Diffie-Hellman bases key exchange on the discrete-log problem. Modern TLS uses elliptic-curve Diffie-Hellman for forward secrecy and RSA or ECDSA for authentication.

title: Renaissance
content: The Renaissance was a cultural movement spanning roughly the 14th through 17th centuries, beginning in Italian city-states and spreading across Europe. It revived classical Greek and Roman learning, advanced humanist scholarship, and saw breakthroughs in painting (Leonardo, Michelangelo, Raphael), printing (Gutenberg), and natural philosophy that prepared the ground for the Scientific Revolution.

title: French Revolution
content: The French Revolution began in 1789 with the storming of the Bastille and the National Assembly's Declaration of the Rights of Man. It overturned the ancien regime, abolished feudal privilege, executed Louis XVI in 1793, and entered a Reign of Terror under Robespierre. Napoleon's coup in 1799 ended the revolutionary phase and inaugurated the Consulate.

title: Industrial Revolution
content: The Industrial Revolution beginning in late-18th century Britain transformed manufacturing through steam power, the factory system, and mechanized textile production. It drove urbanization as agricultural workers moved to mill towns, expanded global trade, and reshaped class structure. Its environmental and labor consequences became central political questions of the 19th century.

title: Fall of the Western Roman Empire
content: The Western Roman Empire fell in 476 when the Germanic chieftain Odoacer deposed Romulus Augustulus. Historians attribute the collapse to a combination of internal decline (political instability, economic strain, military overextension, plague) and external pressure from migrating peoples — Goths, Vandals, Huns, Franks — whom Rome could neither absorb nor repel. The Eastern Empire, centered on Constantinople, persisted for another millennium as Byzantium.

title: Cold War
content: The Cold War was the geopolitical contest between the United States and the Soviet Union from the late 1940s to 1991. It was fought through proxy wars (Korea, Vietnam, Afghanistan), nuclear arms racing, espionage, and ideological competition between liberal capitalism and Marxism-Leninism. Its end with the dissolution of the Soviet Union reshaped the global order into a brief unipolar moment dominated by the United States.
